title: Communication and Query Complexity of Bipartite Perfect Matching
speaker: Yuval Efron
abstract: In this talk, I'll discuss a recent result where we settle the complexities of the maximum-cardinality bipartite matching problem (BMM) up to poly-logarithmic factors in five models of computation:  The two-party communication, AND query, OR query, XOR query, and quantum edge query models.  Our results answer open problems that have been raised repeatedly since at least three decades ago [Hajnal, Maass, and Turan STOC’88; Ivanyos, Klauck, Lee, Santha, and de Wolf FSTTCS’12; Dobzinski, Nisan, and Oren STOC’14; Nisan SODA’21] and tighten the lower bounds shown by Beniamini and Nisan

proof_stages.label: Perfect matching problem and complexity models
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the decision version of bipartite perfect matching and defines the two-party edge-partition communication model together with OR/AND/XOR query variants.

proof_stages.label: Prior bounds and announced target results
proof_stages.summary: Known randomized and deterministic lower bounds and augmenting-path upper bounds leave a gap, and the speaker announces a deterministic O(n log^2 n)-bit protocol plus query-model consequences.

proof_stages.label: Center-of-gravity cutting template for polytope emptiness
proof_stages.summary: A general promise problem asks whether a cube intersected with half-spaces is empty or has volume at least f, and the centroid-cut method solves it in O(log(1/f)) iterations by using violated half-spaces to shrink volume by a constant factor.

proof_stages.label: Primal matching LP attempt and its failure
proof_stages.summary: The edge-variable LP relaxation is shown, via Hall's theorem, to detect nonexistence of perfect matchings, but even after adding slack to obtain positive volume its dimension and distributed edge variables make the resulting cutting protocol too expensive and hard to implement.

proof_stages.label: Dual vertex-cover polytope and low-dimensional volume gap
proof_stages.summary: Taking the LP dual gives a vertex-cover polytope with only 2n variables, whose emptiness is reversed relative to perfect matching and whose nonempty case has enough volume for only O(n log n) centroid iterations.

proof_stages.label: Deterministic edge-communication protocol
proof_stages.summary: Alice and Bob maintain a mutually known graph and centroid polytope; at each step they locally search for an edge constraint violated by the centroid, communicate one such edge using O(log n) bits, and after O(n log n) iterations decide—and in fact find—a perfect matching if it exists.

proof_stages.label: Query and related model consequences
proof_stages.summary: The same violating-edge search is simulated by an OR query, randomized XOR queries can simulate the OR-query protocol, quantum edge protocols and AND-query lower bounds are placed in context, and the Q&A expands the AND lower bound through an intersection/set-disjointness communication formulation.

proof_stages.label: Open directions and barriers beyond the protocol
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys unresolved questions about demand queries, round complexity, general graph matching, max flow, monotone circuit complexity, and hypergraph matching, emphasizing where the centroid/LP approach stops applying or where current bounds are far apart.

examples.name: Unique perfect matching gives zero volume
examples.note: The speaker points out that a graph with only one perfect matching makes the primal matching polytope just a point of zero volume, motivating the slackened n-1/3 constraint used to create positive volume.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the unique-perfect-matching case as making the LP polytope a single point of zero volume and then uses that to motivate the relaxed constraint fix.

passes.reason: a unique perfect matching collapses the LP polytope to a single zero-volume point, obstructing the naive volume approach
